text: The surgeon opens the left posterior rectus sheath, making an incision approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba. The preperitoneal dissection is visible in the midline behind the diastatic linea alba, as the surgeon progresses towards the pelvis.

The surgeon cuts through the arcuate line and transverse fascia, addressing smaller hernias around the umbilicus. The preperitoneal plane is exposed, with fat being removed. The surgeon is positioned anterior to the medial umbilical ligaments, which have been divided, and proceeds to the right lower quadrant, entering the hernia sac to inspect its contents.

The surgeon begins dissection around the caudal end of the hernia, dividing the hernia sac at the neck due to difficulty in preservation. The pseudosac is recruited to patch a posterior defect in the large hernia, facilitating closure of the posterior layer.

The surgeon manipulates the pseudosac within the subcutaneous tissue layer, ensuring adequate thickness for safe dissection. The visual field includes the abdominal space with visible omentum, indicating the ETEP approach.

The surgeon performs dissection in the right groin, carefully separating the peritoneum from the vas deferens and testicular vessels. This maneuver aids in mobilizing the peritoneum to close the posterior defect and initiates the transversus abdominis release (TAR) from the bottom-up direction.

The surgeon opens the preperitoneal fat and fascia, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle covered by transversalis fascia. Scar tissue is carefully opened, and fat is pulled off the transversalis fascia to create a preperitoneal tunnel, ensuring access behind the posterior rectus sheath and confirming the correct lateral plane.

The surgeon identifies a segment of the colon adhered to the lateral part of the hernia sac and proceeds to lyse the adhesions. Polypropylene mesh from a previous surgery is visible inside the abdominal cavity, and the surgeon carefully separates the colon from the mesh.

The surgeon opens the right retrorectus space by incising the right posterior rectus sheath. To achieve adequate cephalad overlap, the ipsilateral and contralateral posterior rectus sheaths are also opened, creating approximately fifteen centimeters of space above the hernia.

The surgeon visualizes the hernia defect within the abdominal cavity, noting its width. The retrorectus space is accessed, and a transversus abdominis release (TAR) is planned to extend laterally from this space to address the hernia defect.

The surgeon cleans the retroactive space, carefully preserving a large neurovascular bundle located medially. The bundle is observed emerging between the internal oblique and transverse abdominis muscles, perforating the posterior lamella of the internal oblique.
The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris and scores the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, utilizing a small hole in the fascia where the neurovascular bundle enters.

The surgeon performs a middle-down transversus abdominis release (TAR) by cutting through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and exposing the transverse abdominis. Scissors are used to lift the tissue, while cautery divides it. The medial neurovascular bundle is carefully preserved by pushing the nerve towards the ceiling and lifting it off the transverse abdominis, utilizing the natural plane between the nerve and the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon pushes the nerve laterally and continues the division of the transverse abdominis underneath it. The transverse abdominis release (TAR) is extended to the cephalad edge of the hernia defect. The procedure progresses into the pre-transversalis fascia space superiorly and the preperitoneal space inferiorly, with the transverse fascia being dissected off the transverse abdominis.
On the left side of the screen, the surgeon transitions from the transverse abdominus release (TAR) to the hernia defect area. At the edge of the hernia, the surgeon enters the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon evaluates the junction between the pre-transversalis and pre-peritoneal spaces, visually distinguishing the anatomical differences between these two areas.

In the upper abdomen, the surgeon navigates the transition from the pre-transversalis space to the pre-peritoneal space. The transverse salus is dissected, revealing abundant pre-peritoneal fat that facilitates the separation of the peritoneum from the transverse salus.

The surgeon performs a bottoms-up TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release) approach, carefully dissecting the preperitoneal fat from the transverse salus fascia. The surgical field reveals a vascular plane, and the surgeon scores the lateral edge of the hernia defect to access the preperitoneal space, encountering an additional fascial layer known as the preperitoneal fascia.

The surgeon demonstrates the transition between the preperitoneal space and the pre-transversalis space, highlighting the anatomical layers involved in the dissection. The view shifts towards the groin area, where the inguinal hernia dissection is performed close to the peritoneum, contrasting with the TAR approach that remains external and anterior to the preperitoneal fascia and fat.

The surgeon cuts through the preperitoneal fascia and preperitoneal fat to combine the pre-transversalis and prepartineal spaces. Due to the patient's large abdomen, the dissection extends laterally to ensure adequate overlap, addressing a defect measuring thirteen centimeters tall and twenty centimeters wide.

The surgeon identifies a large piece of composite mesh within the hernia sac, noting it as a Bard Ventral-X mesh with a polypropylene and Gore-Tex composition. The mesh, originally measuring 20 by 25 centimeters, appears significantly shrunken.
The surgeon excises a firm piece of mesh, identified as having a chronic seroma on CT scan, and places it aside for later extraction. The posterior layer is then closed.

The surgeon closes a large posterior defect by re-approximating tissue between the right-sided lower TAR and the pseudo-sac using a 3-0 V-Loc suture. The pseudo-sac appears stretchy, allowing a small piece to cover a significant area.
The surgeon attempts to close the defect in the posterior rectus sheath, deciding to delay the extraction of the mesh until the closure is confirmed.
The surgeon uses image inversion to simplify suturing the defect, employing forehand throws similar to closing a laparotomy incision.

The pneumoperitoneum is maintained at eight millimeters mercury as the surgeon gradually approximates the edges of the defect using sutures, leaving a small gap. A four-centimeter incision is made at the site of the old scar to extract the mesh.
The surgeon completes the robotic closure of the remaining defect after removing the mesh, using sutures to bring the tissue edges together.

The surgeon inserts a medium weight macropores polypropylene mesh, measuring 37 cm by 30 cm, into the surgical site. The mesh is unfurled from the patient's right side to the left side using a combination of pulling and pushing techniques, aided by rolled-in sutures.

The surgeon demonstrates a new technique for mesh placement by curling the mesh and positioning its ventral surface against the groin and lateral abdominal wall. The mesh is secured with three 3-0 Vicryl sutures to prevent lateral shifting, then unfurled and laid flat to conform to the lateral abdominal wall and pelvic side wall.

The surgeon places the final suture down to the Cooper's ligament area. As the abdomen desufflates, the peritoneum rises, pushing the mesh against the lateral abdominal wall, lateral pelvic wall, and anterior abdominal wall, ensuring coverage of the inguinal and flank regions.
